headline: Welcome Home Ohio awards $2.15M to Toledo-area housing orgs — City, Land Bank, and Habitat share $26.9M statewide round
paragraphs: Governor Mike DeWine and Ohio Department of Development Director Lydia Mihalik announced on May 29 that $26.9 million in Welcome Home Ohio grants will be distributed across 57 counties. Three Toledo-area organizations received awards: the City of Toledo received $600,000 to rehabilitate or construct six homes; the Lucas County Land Reutilization Corporation (Land Bank) received $655,500 — $174,000 for property purchases and $481,500 for rehabilitation or construction of five homes; and Maumee Valley Habitat for Humanity received $900,000 for property purchases ($100,000) and rehabilitation or construction of eight homes ($800,000). Toledo-area organizations received approximately $2.155 million of the statewide pool.

The Welcome Home Ohio program supports the purchase, rehabilitation, and construction of homes and is administered through the Ohio Department of Development. The Lucas County Land Bank and Maumee Valley Habitat for Humanity have an established partnership through which Habitat homebuyers can purchase rehabilitated properties with 0% interest mortgages. WTOL reported the northwest Ohio awards on May 29.
why_it_matters: The Welcome Home Ohio awards land at an inflection point in Toledo's housing strategy — the week after Lucas Metropolitan Housing opened Collingwood Green V and the Port Authority announced intentions to finance renovations at two more HUD-subsidized complexes. The three awards represent three distinct tools operating in parallel: the City deploying direct construction dollars, the Land Bank clearing and rehabbing properties through its acquisition pipeline, and Habitat delivering owner-occupied units at scale with the 0%-interest mortgage model. Together they address the gap David Mann flagged at the Land Bank's 15-year milestone: investors prefer rentals, so publicly-backed programs have to keep pushing homeownership supply. The eight Habitat homes represent eight families converting from renter to owner in Lucas County — the cumulative effect is what moves the needle on the ownership-rate gap over time.
